"Ye shall therefore keep my statutes, and my judgments: which if a man do, he shall live in them: I am the LORD." On "ye shall therefore keep my statutes, and my judgments: which if a man do, he shall live in them," interpreters expound the great saying. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note doing and living — "which if a man do, he shall live in them": the man who keeps God's statutes shall live in them; the witnesses note the significance — the principle of the law (the law promising life to the one who does it, "the man which doeth those things shall live by them"); the lesson — the great Pauline crux: Paul cites this verse for the principle of the law as a way of righteousness ("Moses describeth the righteousness which is of the law, That the man which doeth those things shall live by them," Romans 10:5; "the law is not of faith: but, The man that doeth them shall live in them," Galatians 3:12), contrasting it with the righteousness of faith; the law says "do and live," but since no man can perfectly do, it cannot give life to the sinner, "for if there had been a law given which could have given life, verily righteousness should have been by the law," Galatians 3:21; the lesson — the law's holy demand of perfect obedience, which condemns the sinner who cannot keep it, and which is fulfilled only in Christ, who alone perfectly did and lived, and gives his righteousness to those who believe (Romans 10:4, "Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to every one that believeth"); the figure — the law of doing and living, holy and good, yet unable to justify the sinner, driving him to Christ who is our life.
